sentences.text: Actually, this was on and off, and people couldn't work.
And according to the Russian proverb, it's [RUSSIAN]-- [RUSSIAN].
You don't-- if you don't work, you don't need.
Somebody has to work.
And took any-- did digging ditches, and I remember once they built a new road.
Especially they didn't need it, but just to give people work.
And we went to-- and they pay not by the time, but how many bucket of cent you dig out.
After one day, I couldn't walk.
I couldn't move.
I couldn't nothing, so I didn't go more to work.
No, no.
To be-- to make money.
And when the-- right in the beginning.
But later, I work with a-- with a fellow on the grass, and-- but people had a hard time, very hard time.
Nothing could be bought because the-- the Russians bought out everything what was in the store-- everything.
The stores were empty.
Any piece of material, any clothing, anything.
Everything was for them something new.
And they sent everything to Russia.
This was 1939.
This was a little bit different than now.
And in 1949--
Excuse me.
Yeah. '49.
They were from '39-- no, no. 19-- OK.
Right after they-- their-- they-- was three counties, three Baltic countries-- Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia.
And they would rule and leave those counties independence-- half-way independence.
They still were over there, but they says they can manage themself.
So I found out that one way for them to-- this is the Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE]-- were also in Wil-- in Vilna, which was the capital of Lithuania.
Later they changed to Kaunas.
The Lithuanian changed to Kaunas.
But this was a Polish town, Vilna.
And so I decided to leave the Russian territory and go to Lithuania.
But you could-- legally, you couldn't go through.
So you have to go to like we call it green border in, in the field.
And this was quite a distance.
And so I decided to go on January-- on December 31st.
Why?
Because the Russian soldiers celebrate the New Year's quite heavy, mainly with vodka and singing and dancing.

And the same thing was on their borders and their fields as they wouldn't be so active and it wouldn't-- So was-- this was arrangement with some people who would-- the-- the guides through the-- so one, one-- two days before, one evening, I went to one place, and he took me to a farmer.
And the farmer, we'd been waiting till the evening when it was dark.
And he-- this was winter.
It was all snow covered very heavy.
It was-- so-- Was it still '39?
This was in '39, December 30-- 31st.
Actually, I said 30, but through the border, we went the night 31st.
And I came to Vilna in January 1st, 1940.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The journey, it was also-- was-- so first night, we went halfway.
And at night, we're also sleeping at the some other farmer.
I don't know where it was, someplace in the woods.
The-- the fellow, the guide, which was the guy who owned the sled and two, yeah, myself and some other fellow.
And it's straw on the sled.
But you could-- it was so cold that we couldn't sit on the straw.
We have to run after.
After you got warm, you jump on the sled.
And you-- for a few minutes, you drove and after you-- back and forth.
And so this was the sec-- first, and we had some warm soup, some food over there.
We had to-- oh.
We had to-- the payment was also unusual.
Half of them, I had to pay in Wilno, in Lutsk.
And other half, I had-- I had only half of the money.
The other half was stolen in a half.
And when we came to the-- to the place, I gave him the--
The other half.
But it was torn to half.
No.
No he has the half.
My friend in Lutsk has the other half, and he's supposed to pay him.
And the only signs that he-- that I am over there was-- was no correspondence, was no communications.
So when he got the other half, he knew that--
I don't remember how much was it, how many rubles or it was, so he brought-- so I-- when I was already leaving, I gave him that.
And he actually, he brought me to the street because I didn't know where it is.

And that time, you have to be careful also because the Lithuanian police was very, very, very tough on the people who illegally-- they knew many people from the Russian territories want to come, illegally immigrated.
And but this was January 1st.
This was a new year, cold.
When you walk on the street, it was like kr-ch-ch.
Very, very-- never in my life I saw cold weather like that over there.
And so I came to Vilna, and I knew already where, where-- over there, some many friends.
And eventually I registered.
They-- later they regis-- I registered as a-- that I deserted Russian territory.
They have special-- but I couldn't work there.
Legally, I couldn't work.
But--
Oh yeah.
I registered a police as a-- as a illegally immigr-- as illegal immigrant.
Many people were like that.
But one thing, I couldn't work.
I wasn't allowed to work.
So was Joint-- American Joint Distribution helped quite a bit and I had many friends.
But people had been illegally selling, buying things.
I wasn't so good in, in a thread like that.
But this was until Russian came again and was Russian regime like before.
This was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometimes later, about-- if this was '40, end of-- end of the 1940.
No.
When I was over there, I was-- but again, found-- and at that time, was you could work if you found work.
And but people started-- emigrated illegally to a different country.
Many people came to Israel.
But to be able to get to Israel, you have to have a certificate from here.
And it was easy to get certificate if you were a-- if you apply to be a student at technical [INAUDIBLE] university, but some family has to do it because they have to pay.
You couldn't get certificate if you didn't pay in advance.
But you have to have also a [NON-ENGLISH], which was documents that you graduate from high school.
In that time, I-- that many [INAUDIBLE] people are people here in Israel who came here because I made-- but those not-- they're false documents, but they actually were.
I knew that they were graduate from high school.
So but I-- it was obvious they couldn't emigrate.
No, because nobody apply for me here and--
Yeah, I did.
I wrote to two, but for some reason, they said they didn't get my letter and--
Pardon me?
Can't hear you.
Pardon?
No.
I didn't get nothing from them, and I didn't know what happened.
In the meantime, Germans came in.
This was in June 1941.
But we knew already that the Germans are coming because the Russian, day and night, kind of was evacuating to Russia.
And they said that-- they said these are just temporary.
We're regrouping.
We're drawing.
This what they call it.
But finally, many, many people went east, you know, running away from-- especially Jews-- to Russia.
But I felt I was running so far, I would not run anymore.
And-- and I stay.
It came, a order.
One of the first order was that Jews couldn't walk on the sidewalk.
Officially said under-- everything was under penalty of death, whatever order was.
And the Lithuanian helped them, the German, If not Lithuania, many Jews would survive and would live.
The-- the Lithuanian were wor-- worst than the Germans.
And the Poles was the same.
This is in their blood.
They hate the Jews.
And they're still hating.
But I'm not telling this as a theory, but I'm telling you this from practice, what I experienced.
Actually, I had many enemies-- Germans, Poles, Lithuanians, Russians, communists.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was-- and I decided, no, I didn't.
I will not walk.
Because I saw what happened in Vienna, and at that time, I didn't have a Jewish look, and I could afford to do that.
And when you see the-- a Jew, and he was hiding, he was-- either was looking forward.
He just eyes down so nobody would recognize him.
But I felt I would not-- I was fresh.
I look straight, sure of himself because when they recognize a Jew, immediately, they kicked, they beat him.
They-- couldn't-- I cannot describe what they did-- just spit.
Later, it was orders.
They couldn't walk-- walk on a sidewalk, only they couldn't on sidewalks.
They have walk on the curb.
Later came orders, every Jew had to wear a yellow star.
I realize that if I wore the yellow star, it will happen to me the same thing.
And I become little more sure of myself.
And I didn't wear it.
But when it came order that all the Jews have to go to ghetto, I knew that there's no escape because I couldn't live anyplace.
So I decided to leave Vilna because many people knew me, and I was afraid that they will recognize me eventually.
And I found out by just by coincident from a fellow that one farmer matter looks up a farm-- look for a farmhand who would know German.
So he has a son and a daughter, and he want to that the men will teach him German because he wanted-- So I got the address, address.
Actually, I know where it-- where it is.
This was a village, Rukojnie, 17 miles from Vilna, west.
But I was a little bit afraid to go through regular route because to go out of town, you have to have either permit or document what they are going for.
But many people have been going for farms for food, to buy food and to get food.
Actually, it wasn't so much buying.
They had been bartering because they have been taking something and gotting-- getting something for the food.
Were you not--
Pardon me?
Oh, I had document.
I had my documents.
No, no.
For some reason I had my personal document with photograph.
And the name-- my name was 100% Polish.
My father's name was-- and I had also birth certificate.
And on the birth certificate, I-- very seldom was a birth certificate where somebody-- but I know my-- know why I don't-- didn't have-- my father was a religious man.
But he-- and his name on document was Jacob, which is-- it was Jekov, which is Polish name, Helenah, which is also Polish name.
My mother's name.
Of course, it was father's and mother's name in the birth certificate.
And so I just used those documents.
And I also had some Lithuanian document because I was working on a housing project.
This is from Ger-- from Lithuanian authorities.
But it wasn't also that I am Jew or not Jew.
So those are the only documents which I had.
But I went not through the main highway, but through the field that I knew going east.
I asked where it to go to the-- and finally, I came to the village.
And I asked-- I said that I am-- as I heard from such and such men-- he wasn't a Jew-- that he's looking.
And this man was the only Gentile who knew that I am a Jew.
Nobody-- is that-- that time, men, Gentiles knew that I'm a Jew, but he knew that I am going-- he gave me the address.
And he knew that I'm-- nobody knew where I went.
So I went to, to the fellow, and I asked for a job.
And I said, all my life, I was a farmer.
He look at my hands, and I saw a smirk on his face.
I said, are you looking at my hands?
They are not farmer's hands, but I said I was studying in Vienna, so I-- but my father had the farm.
So every time during the summer time, I was always working on his farm because this was my home.
And I was-- and I-- therefore, I know everything about farming.
And the reason why I went to Vienna because he was a well-- a rich farmer.
And I once also in a subsidium.
I got many subsidium, and I therefore-- for I didn't want to lie because I was afraid if I were lie too much, next time I would not remember what I lied.
So I-- so under those circumstances, he said, let's try it.
For me, was no other way.
I couldn't come back to Vilna.
And I slept in the room on a s-- straw sack, and this was my-- my-- my quarters.
And I-- only for board and room.
No money.
I worked this-- it was-- during the war, people, for board and room.
It's-- It's expensive.
Pardon me?
It's expensive.
Not expensive.
Like people are not volunteering, so at that time, I didn't have other choice, you know?
As long as I have food to eat, and I have some place where I can put my head, that's enough.
And on Sunday afternoon, a priest from the parish came because he told him-- He told him or he found out that the fellow from city who knows German.
Came and he claims that he heard that I'm-- that I know German.
And if I would be willing to help him and to go to German authorities.
And they nationalized the church and all the territories because before the-- the church-- the only income from the church was from the plates which have been giving.
And they had lots of land, and they been leasing those-- the land to farmers.
So during the communistic time, they nationalized the church, and they nationalized all of the land.
And they gave away to the peasant all the land.
So now he wants denationalized so that he would get the land back.
So I couldn't say no, so I said yes.
Why wouldn't say-- why would--
So I say, yeah, we can-- told him we will go next day in the morning.
So I agree, and he's supposed to come with the wagon and-- and the [NON-ENGLISH] and the driver.
It was a the seat on the front and little small wagon with two seats on the back with the big wheels, black wagon.
And-- and we're supposed to go to the city.
But he said, what-- where we should go, what city with authority, with the office?
I said, I don't know, but we have to go to silent civilian authorities.
What civilian authorities, we didn't know.
But I-- I realized that the civilian authorities would be the same as before the German came in.
But the next morning, instead the priest, came only the driver.
He said the priest is sick and wondered if I would be willing to go myself and represent the priest.
Naturally, immediately, I said yes.
Why wouldn't say no.
I couldn't say no.
I don't have any other excuse.
And we-- and I went.
And we went to the-- but instead civilian, it was all German army and Poles with the rifles and walking.
And they didn't let me in.
But I spoke fluently German, and I convinced them that I came here as representative of the parish, and this is a big parish, and the whole village, and this is on official business.
This is not something for the German office to know.
So he let me in.
When I walk in, I didn't know where-- which office to go.
But I-- first door, I was-- it was in German and in Lithuanian.
A civilian affairs-- adminis-- administrative affairs.
So I say I want to [INAUDIBLE].
So I came, walk in the office.
Knock on door, walk in the office.
And it was-- by the desk was a German officer, and in the corner, a secretary, also in uniform, a blond German girl.
And I told him what I came here for.
And he voiced-- a loud voice, said, you know we are in a war with the communists and you came here with such piddly things.
And I don't know I had the guts.
I never pound in my life on a table-- never, never.
And I didn't pound, but just put my hand on the table and told him, and you want me to come to those people and tell him that you don't want to do-- to denationalize this what communists did it?
They will call me communist, and they will call you communist.
I cannot come back to the-- to the village.
I cannot.
So and he saw my serious-- that I--
But-- but when I put my hand on his desk, I knew that he was-- that he was ready to take out the gun and shoot me or something.
So immediately, I start talking about the communists.
And if I wouldn't did that, he would threw me out.
I don't know what he would do with me because I was dressed like a peasant.
And the only thing, I had the 3/4 boots like-- like the German was wearing.
And-- and so he asked, what do you want?
So I told him.
So he dictates something to the secretary.
She typed on a typewriter.
This was hand write typer, no electric typer.
She will type very fast and gave it to him.
And he read it.
He said, this what you wanted?
I says, this is perfect. .
And gave it to me and naturally put the signature, put the German swastika big stamp, which was-- I said, oh, this is what I wanted.
And gave it to me and said, and I don't want to see you ever again here.
And I put my feet like a German soldier together, salute him, and said, no, you will never see me again, and I walk out.
And walk out and went to the place where he was parking the in a backyard of some house someplace and-- and I said we can go back.
So went back and came to the priest and gave him the document.
Oh, this is like a from heaven and Jesus must be with me, he said, because I got something.
And I didn't say anything.
I didn't make any comment.
Never I made any comments.
And he said, what do we do with this now?
So I said, I don't know.
You ask for that.
So he said he really didn't know because he was very, very crude man.
He was-- he didn't very well-educated.
I don't know what he got education, where he was born, but from his speech, you could tell also that [INAUDIBLE] men.
And we went-- so I told him again we had to go Lithuanian civilian the voivodeship and present this.
So he didn't know Lithuanian, and asked me if I would do-- represent him.
So I went next day.
And came to the fellow and show it to him.
He doesn't know German, so I translate to him in Lithuanian what it is.
And so he took this, and he said, oh, we'll do it.
I said, when?
When-- when-- we'll do it and didn't commit himself.
And so I said, no, you have to do it tomorrow.
This is the German authority's order.
So instead of me being afraid of him, he was afraid of me because he was thinking that I am German or some connection with paper or documents or swastika at this time.
He'd never see at that time.
That was right in the beginning.
So the-- So and I said, this document, I cannot leave you because this belongs to the priest.
So he asked to make a document, a copy.
Naturally, I said, I don't-- I can make you a copy.
So he gave me a pencil and a piece of paper.
And in Lithuanian, I-- I couldn't well-- I didn't know Lithuanian well, so-- but I could translate that and-- and left him.
And I said, I expect tomorrow that this will be done.
And it's true.
Next day, he sent two militia men to those peasants and told them what-- that this doesn't belong anymore to them, and it belongs to the church.
And also in documents, I ask him to enter that this is [NON-ENGLISH].
This is the same status quo as it was before the war.
And this is-- and also the-- but to not to make the peasant mad, the priest leased them the land like before, but he didn't took away.
But the same thing like was, but it was his property, and he lease them the land.
